summarization.a: Top and side views of pristine graphene, showing the honeycomb lattice structure with all carbon atoms in sp² hybridization. Unit cells are indicated, and atom types are color-coded.
summarization.b: Top and side views of graphene with a monovacancy defect, illustrating the missing carbon atom and the resulting local reconstruction in the lattice.
summarization.c: Top and side views of graphene with a 585 divacancy defect, showing two adjacent missing carbon atoms and the formation of a 5–8–5 ring configuration.
summarization.d: Top and side views of graphene containing a 558-grain boundary (GB), highlighting the periodic arrangement of 5- and 8-membered rings along the boundary.
summarization.e: Top and side views of graphene with an oxygenated grain boundary, showing O atoms bonded at the GB sites and structural modifications due to oxidation.
summarization.f: Top and side views of graphene with a hydrogenated grain boundary, showing H atoms terminating dangling bonds at the GB, stabilizing the structure for ALD modeling.

a.question: Do topological defects in graphene enable local nucleation of Pt during atomic layer deposition (ALD)?
a.answer: No. Pristine graphene without defects does not provide active sites for Pt ALD nucleation due to the absence of dangling bonds.

b.question: Which graphene defect type is reported to have the highest affinity for hydrogen adatoms among the models shown?

d.question: Explain how the structural models in Figure 2 help in understanding area-selective Pt deposition on graphene during ALD.
d.answer: The structural models illustrate how pristine graphene lacks active sites for ALD due to its intact sp² network, while defects such as vacancies and grain boundaries introduce dangling bonds and modified electronic structures. These features enhance precursor adsorption and chemisorption, enabling localized Pt nucleation. By comparing different defect types and chemical terminations, the models help explain why Pt deposition occurs selectively at defective regions, guiding strategies for controlled, area-selective metal growth on graphene.

c.question: List the graphene structural models illustrated in Figure 2 and explain their relevance to ALD reactivity.
c.answer: Pristine graphene: Perfect sp² lattice with no dangling bonds, low ALD reactivity, Monovacancy graphene: Missing carbon atom creates dangling bonds and high local reactivity, 585 divacancy graphene: Reconstructed defect that disrupts π-conjugation, 558 grain boundary: Line defect with enhanced adsorption affinity, Oxygenated grain boundary (epoxy): Oxygen functionalization increases chemical activity, Hydrogenated grain boundary: Surface termination alters adsorption behavior
